title: Mulled wine
subtitle: Red wine gently warmed with orange, cinnamon, and star anise.
excerpt: A bottle of red wine heated slowly with orange slices, cinnamon, star anise, cloves, and sugar. Do not boil. Serve in heatproof glasses or mugs. The smell alone is worth it.
sourceNotes: Mulled wine has been drunk in Britain since at least the medieval period, when spiced heated wines were common across northern Europe as a way to make inferior wine palatable and to provide warmth in cold conditions. The German Glühwein tradition has had particular influence on the British version since the rise of Christmas markets from the 1990s onwards. The spice combination of cinnamon, star anise, and cloves is now the British standard.

recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch for gatherings. Keep warm in the pan over a very low heat; do not allow to boil.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be made up to an hour ahead and kept warm on the lowest hob setting.

content.text: The only rule with mulled wine is not to boil it. Boiling burns off the alcohol and concentrates the tannins, producing a harsh, bitter drink. It should reach a temperature where it is just too hot to sip comfortably: around 70 °C if you have a thermometer, or simply 'steaming without bubbling' if you do not.
Use a wine that is drinkable but not precious: something full-bodied and relatively inexpensive. The spices and sugar do most of the flavour work. A cheap Malbec or Spanish red works well.
A large saucepan and a ladle. Heatproof glasses or mugs for serving.

Pour {{red-wine}} of red wine into a large saucepan. Add the orange slices, cinnamon sticks, star anise, cloves, and {{light-brown-sugar}} of sugar. Set over a low-medium heat and warm slowly, stirring to dissolve the sugar. Do not allow to boil.

Once steaming, reduce the heat to its lowest setting and leave to steep for 15 minutes to develop the spice flavours. Add the brandy if using. Taste and add more sugar if needed. Ladle into heatproof glasses or mugs, adding an orange slice to each.

items.symptom: Mulled wine tastes harsh and tannic
items.cause: Overheated or boiled at some point.
items.fix: A low, steady heat is essential. If it came to the boil, the tannic quality cannot be undone — it can be partly masked with more sugar and orange juice, but the wine is compromised.

items.cause: Not steeped for long enough, or too little spice.
items.fix: Steep for at least 20 minutes. Add another cinnamon stick and an extra star anise.

items.cause: Sugar added before tasting, or wine was already sweet.
items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice to sharpen it. Taste the wine first and start with half the sugar quantity.

items.cause: Mulled at too high a temperature for too long.
items.fix: Keep the heat low enough that the surface is steaming but not simmering. The brandy, added at the end, partially restores the alcoholic warmth.

content.text: Mulled wine is the drink that appears at the moment the Christmas season begins in Britain: at markets, at office parties, at the first cold weekend of November. The tradition of heating wine with spices is old enough that a version appears in medieval cookery manuscripts, but the specific combination that is now standard owes something to the German Glühwein tradition that entered British culture through the Christmas market boom of the 1990s and 2000s. It is the smell as much as the taste that matters.
